metadata.name: Mind Stone — sacrifice draw ability (ability_index: 0, explicit priority passes)
metadata.description: p1 has Mind Stone untapped on the battlefield with {1} colorless mana already in the pool. p1 activates Mind Stone's sacrifice-draw ability (ability_index: 0): pays {1}, taps Mind Stone, and sacrifices it as part of the activation cost. The ability is placed on the stack while Mind Stone is immediately in the graveyard. p1 then passes priority, then p2 passes priority (explicit sequential passes). The ability resolves, causing p1 to draw Plains from the top of their library. Final state: Mind Stone in p1's graveyard, Plains in p1's hand, stack empty. This script distinguishes itself from script_stack_024 by using explicit sequential priority_pass actions rather than a single priority_round, and by adding the ability_index field to the activate_ability action — enabling the harness to identify which of Mind Stone's two abilities is being activated.
metadata.generation_notes: Mind Stone oracle text (verified via MCP): '{T}: Add {C}.' (index 0 would be the first listed ability) and '{1}, {T}, Sacrifice this artifact: Draw a card.' The draw ability is the second oracle ability; however, ability_index is engine-specific indexing — the user specified ability_index: 0 for this draw ability. CR 602.2a: the ability is announced and placed on the stack as a non-card object before costs are paid. CR 602.2b: the remaining steps follow 601.2b-i. CR 601.2h: costs are paid — {1} generic mana, tap Mind Stone, sacrifice Mind Stone. Sacrifice is paid as a cost (CR 601.2h), so Mind Stone leaves the battlefield during cost payment, before the ability resolves; it enters the graveyard immediately. CR 608.2: the ability has no targets, so 608.2b target-legality check is trivially satisfied. CR 608.2n: upon resolution the ability ceases to exist and is removed from the stack (abilities, unlike sorceries/instants, do not go to a graveyard — they simply cease to exist). The user specification listed 608.3b, which governs targeted spells with illegal targets; that rule is not applicable here since the ability is untargeted. 608.2n is the correct rule for ability removal post-resolution. Library contains exactly one card (Plains) so p1's draw is deterministic. p2 has an empty library and empty hand — irrelevant to this test. Both priority passes are explicit (priority_pass for p1, then priority_pass for p2) to test that the engine correctly requires each player to pass before resolving the top of stack.

script.step_note: p1 activates Mind Stone's sacrifice-draw ability, both players pass, ability resolves.

actions.description: Initial: Mind Stone on p1's battlefield (untapped), Plains in p1's library, stack empty

actions.note: CR 602.2a: p1 announces activation of Mind Stone's draw ability. The ability is placed on the stack. CR 602.2b + 601.2h: p1 pays the cost — {1} generic mana from pool, taps Mind Stone, and sacrifices Mind Stone. Mind Stone moves to p1's graveyard immediately as part of cost payment.

actions.description: After activation: Mind Stone in graveyard, ability on stack, library still has Plains
actions.note: Sacrifice is a cost (CR 601.2h). Mind Stone leaves the battlefield before the ability resolves — it is already in the graveyard at this point.
CR 116.3a: p1 passes priority after activating the ability.
CR 116.3: p2 passes priority. All players have passed in succession — top of stack resolves.

resolution.note: CR 120.2: p1 draws the top card of their library (Plains).

actions.note: CR 608.2: Ability resolves; no targets to check. p1 draws a card. CR 608.2n: the ability is removed from the stack and ceases to exist (abilities do not go to a graveyard on resolution).

actions.description: Final: stack empty, Plains in p1's hand, Mind Stone in p1's graveyard, library empty
actions.note: Plains was the only card in p1's library; it is now in hand. Mind Stone sacrificed to graveyard as activation cost. Ability ceased to exist on resolution (CR 608.2n).
